result:
There's a peculiar silence that exists only in the early morning, when the world hasn't quite remembered it's supposed to be noisy. I think we miss these intervals—those slender gaps between the clamor of obligations—because we're trained to fill emptiness with content, any content, as if stillness might swallow us whole if we let it linger too long.

I sat on a porch last Tuesday watching mist dissolve over the neighbor's oak. Not productive. Not educational. Not even especially beautiful by conventional standards. Just mist, just oak, just the slow unspooling of dawn. And in that twenty minutes, I felt more restored than I had in weeks of optimized schedules and wellness apps. We talk about "being present" as if it's another task to master, another checkbox, when really it's the opposite of achievement. It's the surrender of measuring altogether.

Maybe that's what draws us to water, to fire, to anything that moves without arriving. A river isn't trying to get anywhere; it simply goes. The flame isn't ascending toward a promotion. There's an honesty in purposeless motion that contradicts everything our culture prizes. We've built temples to efficiency and forgotten that wastefulness—true, glorious wastefulness of time and attention—is where joy hides.

So here's my quiet rebellion: I will watch the coffee cool. I will walk the long way home. I will let the silence stay silent, refusing the urge to narrate it, improve it, or capture it for an audience. Some things only belong to the person experiencing them. Let this be one.
